out there who are so good at their dang
jobs and you're certainly one of them
that their signal to word spoken Ratio
or their signal per minute of content
ratio is off the charts man uh you're a
phenomenal educator I'm so grateful to
have you on the program I'm sure our
audience will uh very much appreciate
this thank you I appreciate the kind
compliment yeah of course my friend so
uh for people who've been sleeping under
the world's most comfortable Rock uh can
you give us a little background about
yourself before we hop in yeah so I am
the uh founder and um Chief uh cook and
head bottle washer at biano Research LLC

28th year in in existence um before that

small firm called Arbor research and
trading which is a small independent
institutional independent brokerage firm
in Chicago in the Chicago area from 1990
to
1998 um Jim Stevens was my partner at uh
our research and he was his idea that I
spin myself off as Bianco research I get
to call myself president of something
I'm the third largest shareholder of
Arbor Arbor is the second largest
shareholder of Bianco research so it's
been 34 years that I've been involved
with them my main thr
is macro and it's kind of with a fixed
income bent because I am still
affiliated with a fixed income brokerage
firm so I talk mainly to um you know uh
Bond portfolio managers Bond Traders and
bond salesmen but I've tried to Branch
off in doing more than just you know
getting into the weeds in the bond
market but I kind of start there and
kind of go more macro uh with a lot of
what I do 100% man and speaking of bonds
uh I I noticed you have um I want to say
Bond ETF is that correct that is correct
uh in 23 what I technically did was I
started a bond index called the Bianco
research Total return index it's BTR
indx If you have a Bloomberg or it's
Bianco advisors.com and then Wisdom Tree
my partner started a ETF that tracks my
index and that's been about 15 months
now that it's been undergoing I'll say
this you know Pro topro to you I'll say
to you
we are in the core Bond category with
our ETF which means that we're total
return we're trying to beat the
Bloomberg aggregate index 470 funds in
the index or in the universe of core
Bond and we were in the 19th percentile
so we outperformed 81% of the people in
our group I am under No Illusion as I
like to say to my professional friends
there will be a year where we be in the
fourth quartel because there always is
and you're naive to think you will never
be but it wasn't the first year so that
really helped me quite a
and getting this thing off the ground so
we've got gotten off to a very good
start with it so thank you bringing it
up no that's amazing man uh really
really proud of you for that man so uh
that is awesome uh I love pulling on
these sort of threads in terms of uh
institutional Asset Management products
what would you say is sort of your core
um I don't want to say secret sauce but
what's your core sort of selling product
relative to the other I want to say
would you say 400 something funds in the
core Bond index yeah so two things I
think we do uh that's kind of unique the
first one is we buy other fixed income
ETFs and I've written a lot about this
um the efficiency of the ETF is such
that it almost is better off to do it
that way I've been in a bond market for
three decades almost four decades and I
was like man these ETFs are so easy
they're so transparent and they're so
cheap to do yeah so we put all of our
ETFs that we own into a uh a portfolio
Management program and it has a little
look through function so we could say
instead of us owning 15 ETFs fixed
income ETFs how many IND how many unique
positions are there aggregated in the 15
ETFs and we analyze it that way there's
18,000 positions between those 15 ETFs
and then you know the question is the
all-in cost for us to own those ETFs is
four basis points if I had to put
together a facility to buy and track
18,000 Securities it cost me a lot more
bis point to do that and so that is you
know and there's so many of these sliced
and diced fixed income ETFs coming out
very narrowly based and stuff there's
hardly a strategy I have not encountered
that I can't do with an ETF and what's
interesting is that is fairly common in
the equity space that there's ETFs that
buy other ETFs you use ETFs to implement
strategies and equities in the last
three to five years it's become much
more prevalent in the um you know in the
fixed income space uh where we where
we've seen it there the other thing I do
which I'll be honest with Pro to proo
here I thought this was going to be a
problem but it turned out to be a virtue
good I manage an index so what we do
when we manage the index is we look at
um the the the the the weightings within
the the fixed income Universe how many
corporates do we want want to have how
many mortgages do we want to have do we
want to bank loans what is our duration
our exposure to interest rates our yield
curve structure these are the kind of
things that we look at and we reset them
once a month with all these
ETFs and we hold it for a month until
the next monthly meeting and I thought
man I'm going to have to only change
this thing once a month and it takes
three days in order to affect the change
that's going to hamper me right because
what happens if the market goes nuts in
the beginning a month or middle of the
month I'm kind of stuck with the
position that I have well I've been
doing this now for 20 months and I think
that is the best thing that's ever
happened and the reason is is it forces
me to think longer term I'm not chasing
the next five basis points in the market
because I can't because of the way that
I've got it structured so every time we
reposition we're like how do we want to
have this positioned for the next 3 to
six months and I'll give you a great
example the best month
we ever had was we were positioning
longterm we had a meeting and we
initiated our positions October 31st I
was like you next week is the election
then we've got the FED meeting then
we've got payrolls and then we've got
CPI and we're going to position a month
ahead of that and we're not going to do
anything during the month of November
regardless of what happens so we had to
basically say forget all that noise
where do we think the longer term is
going to go and it turned out it was our
best month ever we were at the second
percentile of those 470 funds for that
for that month and we did it all
beforehand and I felt like this is much
better because we've had some struggling
months too in there and it's like and I
can like be restful on that look I can't
do anything until the end of the month
so the Market's moving against me it is
what it is I don't sit there staring up
at night going should I should I
reposition should I change something in
some way I can't so now it's all about
forget about what's happening in the
next 2 weeks think longer term where do
I want to be for the bulk of the year
into 2026 and so that has really helped
so like I said when I went into this I
thought man this is going to be so
limiting that you can only change it
once a month and I like no it's so
clarifying it forces me to think the
proper way cuz I've been in my research
business I've talked to thousands of men
managers and too many of them get so
sucked in the chasing the next 3% in the
stock market or the next 5% in the bond
market they they they're so lost in the
forest and they forget what they're
trying to do because they're just like
how do I position before the payroll
report what should I do before this
earnings report comes out I was like you
are trying to manage money into 2026 and
you're getting yourself all bent out of
shape about nvidia's earnings report or
something like that and so this forces
that kind of Clarity and long-term
thinking on you and at least for the
first 15 months we've been doing it
we've had enormous success with it I
have no doubt in my mind that enormous
success is going to continue because it
sounds like you've institutionalized a
process that put its guard rails around
many of the common behav characteristics
that that sort of keep most investors
from achieving their Strategic
investment objectives in fact uh I'm
actually reading that from a chart Colin
if you put this on WE featured these two
slides in every single piece of our
research because what we want to do uh
in addition to helping our clients make
money in asset markets and and save
money in asset markets heading into draw
Downs is we want to help them become
better investors and sort of there's
three things that popped into my head as
you were explaining that process uh
three of these characteristics that
popped into my head number one the
action bias we all overtrade too much so
forcing that that institutionalized
process of you know your investment
committee meeting only being monthly
sort of reduces your your ability to
sort of overtrade your portfolio uh
there's confirmation by there four of
really availability horis and
confirmation bias if the Market's moving
your way you're probably going to want
to do too much more of that however if
you have a much slower uh uh sort of um
investment committee process you allows
yourself to incorporate valuations and
and price targets a little bit better
availability heuristic obviously we're
talking about the FED meeting the
delection all this other stuff that you
can see right in front of your nose that
feels like it's a real big Market
Catalyst that a lot of folks position
for uh but there's obviously what's
going to happen in the first half of
this year what's gonna happen in 2025
that may have a significant impact and
my favorite
this is this is my favorite is the
illusion of validity uh which is the
bias that effectively says we all think
we know more than we know and and I
think having a process that forces you
to you know just sort of I would say
sort of be along with for the ride
alongside the market gives you more time
to learn more things such that the ratio
of I think I know it all in my head
versus I actually don't know at all let
me learn more that ratio gets
right-sized over time and so uh really
proud of you for building that what's
the ticker for that etto wtbn Wisdom

just to add into what you were saying
you're exactly right on a lot of this

or corporate bonds I mean we'll get into

these views but I'm not nailing to the
Mast head I've got the secret of what
the inflation rate's going to do from
now to the end of time or got the secret
of what interest rates are going to do
from now to the end of time and so this
process allows me to say here's our
belief on where inflation is going to be
or here's our belief on what interest
rates are going to do or Corp bonds are
going to do and then we spend the month
constantly challenging ourselves we have

and we start thinking about it it and

wouldn't say a lot of them maybe a third

views uh with respect to uh let's pivot
to the bond market first since we're

not you know one certainly amongst the
talking head community of the hire for
longer narrative and and and not just
importantly early adopters but really
having a credible fundamental framework
that um you know sort of uh argued very
cogently and coherently why we were HED
for longer not just that we were HED for
longer as a function of the current
cyclical Dynamics in the economy I think
you understand the change in the line of
the structural Dynamics in the econom
economy uh uh better than most so can we
talk let's sort of unpack that and and
pull on that thread a bit where are we
in this higher for longer uh thesis um
you know is it going to be the linear
straight line probably not but but
generally speaking what's your take on
where we are in respect to the bond
market let's say for 2025 yeah so two
things um whenever I start with bigger
picture whenever you have a recession or
financial crisis this is a shock to the
system we had both in 2020 the shutdown
and the and the recession and the big
fall in the markets and the
extraordinary action by the FED y coming
out of a episode like that 2008 before
that 911 before that the economy changes
the financial markets change and I want
to be careful with this word change
change is not dystopian I didn't say
they get worse they change they're
different and so coming into 2020 we
were in a long period of a 40-year bull
market in bonds that started in 1981 we
were in a in a period of very low
interest rates um excuse me very low
inflation that was helping to keep bonds
keep going lower and lower and lower the
shock of 2020 I've said I think that
ended that cycle and so I started off
with with point one just like 08 ended a
cycle the financial crisis of' 08 we
went into the period of quantitative
easing right after that when we never
had that before I said I thought that
this was going to end that period of low
inflation that allowed quantitative
easing and that we were going to see a
series of imbalances as we come out of
the economy or come out of the the
lockdowns and we largely have the
biggest argument I would give you on the
change when I said it's change change is
not worse is remotor about a third of
the population right now works remotely
that means remotely is defined as some
period of time one to four days a week
um where you get paid to do your job off
of a central location either an office a
site a hospital you know or or something
like that um work from home would be if
you did it 5 days a week at home so
remote work has fundamentally changed
the labor market it's fundamentally
changed the way that we live our lives
there's an imbalance that we saw we all
understand coming out of this the first
imbalance we saw was the supply chain
squeeze which drove inflation up to 9%
and that's come down but what we're
seeing now is we're settling out I
believe at a higher level of inflation
somewhere around 3 to 4% not under two
why do that matter because that means in
the fair value numbers in the bond
market mean the FED might be coming to
the realization that the neutral funds
rate might be closer to the high 3es or
4% not three or 2% that means that the
neutral rate for the 10e for the
two-year yield might be around four and
a half% that means the 10-year yield
might be in the low fives it's about 455
right now as we're talking so these rate
levels are not very high relative to
what we should expect they are high
relative to what we've seen over the
last 15 years but that was a different
cycle and so along those lines whenever
I see chairman Paul speak he always has
this phrase that he goes we see the
economy broadly Mor moralizing we see
the economy returning to prepandemic
levels and I always kind of yell at the
TV when I listen to them I go that's the
old Cy
this is a new cycle this it's different
we're not going back to that old cycle
and again don't mistake different for
dystopian or worse uh it was a banana
and now it's an orange is an orange
better than a banana no it depends on
your point of view um is remote work
better or worse it depends on if you're
the boss or if you're the employee I
guess as to how you VI it and so I
started with that and that that stickier
level of inflation is where it's going
to go the second thing I pointed out was
simple Bond math early on which is why I
was very bearish on bonds if I was right
about this inflation level and rates
were going to go up the math of getting
interest rates off of zero to 5% which
is essentially where the Bloomberg us
aggregate index is it's around 5% yield
was going to be enormously painful it
was going to be big declines in prices
to get the yields up and as a matter of
fact there's a professor at Santa clar
University named ed mcy and he has Bond
indices going back 200 plus years wow
and what we've seen over the last three
or four years has been one of the worst
periods for the bond market in well over
a hundred years uh it is because we had
to go from zero to five the other thing
I've been recently counseling about is
be careful of momentum thinking there's
so much momentum thinking in these
markets right this what's the stock
market going to do this year it's going
to go up 20% why because it's gone up
20% the last couple years what about
bonds bonds are the most uninvestable
asset class that have ever existed why
because the last three years have been a
disaster and as far as the bond market I
said yes they have been because we had
to get zero we had to get off of zero
and we had to get the five but now that
we're at five the odds or the
probabilities that you're going to see
continued disaster in the bond market is
going to be very low to give you one
quick math experiment
I said from 0 to 5 according to Ed MCC
Cory's data over the last three years if
you held long-term bonds bonds of 10
years or longer you've lost 19% of your
money worse than 100 years what would it
take now that we're at 5% yields to lose
19% again over the next three years we'd
have to go to 16% on y wow and we're not
going to 16% or as I once jok to
somebody I said if we did go up to 16%
your 20% loss in bonds would be your
best investment because everything else
would be pmel to death at that the point
is the point is the bond math says to
you that all of that pain should be
behind us the the the duration of bonds
or the sensitivity to interest rates has
come down a lot so that when interest
rates move the prices don't move as much
and now there's a 5% coupon throwing you
off income as opposed to 0 1% like we
had three years ago so the structure of
the market has changed and again so many
people are so momentum driven right they
think well stock market goes up 20%
every year cuz it has bitcoin's going to
double again cuz it has you know they
think gold is going to have another 25%
year because it did and I was like
that's not necessarily a good way to
look at it my favorite meme I've been
using on this is from the movie Top Gun
um you know the original Top Gun where
where the the when he's there and he
said Maverick that is some of the best
flying I've ever seen right up until the
moment you were killed momentum
investing is great you are a hero and
you do great with momentum investing
until that plane hits the canyon wall
and you are done then at that point
that's what you got to be careful of
when you're a momentum investor and
that's what a lot of people are starting
to tell me no we're give another 20%
year in the stock market and we're give
25% year in the gold market and you know
and so far for most of January see I
told you Brian our way to another 20%
year yeah but at some point that Canyon
wall is going to come up on you if
you're not careful and you're going to
have a real problem 100% man uh Master
Class man so a few things I'll say on
the momentum 100% agree if you're a
momentum investor and don't have signals
to tell you to stop doing whatever it is
that you previously were doing you're
not investing with momentum properly
I'll just
say Google me if you need more color on
that we don't have time to unpack why I
believe that you know what the other the
just the other joke about momentum
investors is right on the trends and
wrong on the ends is what they are but
the problem is those wrong on the ends
Parts can be really painful if you're
careful you know even though you get
that middle part you you know you think
you're a genius in that middle part
that's what you got to be really careful
about 100% our clients know this full
well this is stuff that we've had very
you know High Lev institutional
conversations with uh them in our
research and in our community chat about
how to elongate the right on the trend
part and shorten the wrong on the end
part right that's we spend a lot of our
our you know research in terms of our
research and development in designing
systems that you know shrink the wrong
on the ends relative to the right on the
trans part so I think we've done a
pretty good job with that but as always
we're optimizing and updating our models
as we do more research to to optimize
these things uh you said uh a couple
things that I want to unpack uh one on
the sort of terminal level of the bond
uh and then two with respect to the
structural changes in the economy let's
start with two because I'm already on
the slide this Colin if you throw the
slide up this is our secular inflation
model we we introduced this back in
January of 2022 uh with the idea very
similar to the idea that that you had
with the sense that uh I don't know if
we're going back to the old economy
certainly when you look at and analyze
as many time series as we do and you do
over at Bianca research and and your
friends over at Arbor as well excellent
data data scientists over there as well
when you analyze as many time series as
we do you can see just visually that we
are at different levels relative to the
the prior regime relative to the the
post crisis post GFC crisis uh era and
so I built a model to quantify the
deviation from that prior regime and it
interpolates the sort of either
unweighted or weighted deviation across
a variety of factors some are weighted
some aren't weighted like fiscal policy
the change in The Sovereign fiscal
balance of GDP the change in
globalization using Imports as a percent
of GDP as a proxy for that looking at
housing Supply household information
divided by existing home Inventory uh um
up at 1.9 productivity has gone up a lot
so that's a disinflationary impulse as
has the Advent of Technology into the
economy that's also disinflationary but
you also have wage inflation running
well above Trend and you also have the
amount of cash that's floating around on
household sector balance sheets that
could be very easily spent into the
economy in a moment's notice uh um
that's a that's a eight Sigma relative
to where we were prior to uh prior to
the pandemic and so our math on this uh
whether you look it on a raw on weighted
basis says the underlying Trend the
equilibrium level of core PC inflation
so the hor Al line in this stylized
diagram is somewhere around 2.9 to 3%
right and this model has been signaling
that since January of 2022 somewhere
between 2.7 I want to say on the low end
since then and 3.2 on the high end but
somewhere around High twos low
threes is that is that crazy to say out
loud because to me when I look at
economic statistics just on a daily
basian inference process basis it seems
like we're moving making progress
towards two but we're also starting to
see a lot of evidence of you know very
relevant time series including many of
these starting to stall out at levels
that would imply we're not getting back
to two what what are your thoughts on
that exactly that I mean we're seeing
the same thing too is that um you know
there's there's an anchoring or you know
bias and that to there's too much
anchoring bias in financial markets
anchoring bias one is you know you'll
hear J Paul say this all the time like
well we've gone from 9 to3 okay we all
knew nine was a supply chain constraint
maybe we didn't know what at the day of
the 9% but we soon learned that that
there was a unique set of circumstances
that caused it to go to 9% inflation and
that that wasn't going to last the real
question is are we getting back to two
and if you look at recent data like for
the second half of 24 it's stalling out
in the you know depending on the measure
you use in the high three I mean in the
high twos to the low 3es this is not 2%
and that is going to be a problem as we
go forward because if you look at
Financial markets you know remember back
two weeks ago before the CPI number when
the 10-year yield was at 480 everybody
was like oh man rates are too high rates
are too high and I kept saying get used
to these rates this is kind of normal
and this is being driven by this higher
level of inflation the other thing to
keep in mind about inflation there was a
Fed Governor Dan trillo he was there
from 2009 to 2017 and I like to joke the
best fed Governor to listen to the ones
that recently leave because then they
tell you what they really think because
basically the job of a Fed Governor is
you're hand it to talking points and you
better read them like you meet them and
then once you leave you can kind of
write your own talking points so he goes
to Brookings Institute this is October
2017 and he says the FED has no working
theory on on inflation and and he was
sincere that they have looked at all the
theories what causes inflation to go up
or down is it is it government spending
is it too much money is it um is it
technology being disinflationary and he
said in isolation you look at all this
stuff and it doesn't correlate and then
he goes on to say because inflation is a
funny kind of economic statistic because
it's somewhat psychological too whether
or not people believe we are in an
inflation period or don't that's why the
F the FED is so obsessed with inflation
expectations so I've often argued it is
the single hardest thing to understand
and a lot of people on Wall Street would
tell me no it's easiest thing to
understand just look at the money supply
number or look at the look at
technological advances or look at
government spending in the short term
they do matter for inflation but the
bigger picture for the psychology of
inflation is really where I think that
we've got the big problem and that
people are starting to believe that
there's some embedded level of inflation
where they didn't before and it's helped
to keep these numbers up and what you're
starting to see what you mentioned I'm
starting to see the same thing too is
that we're starting to to bottom out
again why does three versus two matter
if you're not deep into this stuff
because if you think 5% tenure yield is
going to be a red headline on the quote
screens and everybody's going to
hyperventilate and get used to it
because that's kind of the world that we
live in right now um we're not
necessarily returning to a 2% inflation
world or to restate it the reason we
would return to 2% inflation is because
we've having a recession and we've
killed demand and you might not like the
reason that we got back to 2% inflation
but short of that I think we're new in a
new higher in rate environment the
40-year bull market in bonds is over the
worst part of the bare Market is done
because we had to get from zero to five
now there's a coupon and now we're going
to start in on the second phase 100% I
couldn't agree more man and and you made
you made a great point with respect to
the pain might be over in the bond
market but that this process really NE
hasn't necessarily concluded you know
when we do the math on TR trying to back
into what is a fair value for let's say
the 10year treasury yield we sort of
look at it through two different
approaches one the sort of deviation
from the long run mean of term Premia uh
and just sort of adding that back uh you
know to the to the bond yield uh Colin
if you thr slide 101 up here this Char
just shows 10year treasury term premum
which is the excess yield you get as an
investor for rolling over short-term
paper instead of taking a liquidity risk
in the bond market and when you subtract
that deviation from the long run mean of
around 150 basis points you we're
talking about 5 and a half percent on a
nominal tenure so you can you argue
whether or not ter premium should be
average or not I would argue for a
couple of reasons that it actually you
can make the case that it should be
above average right now uh one we have
higher rates of inflation that are
leading to positive excess inflation
relative to the F Target so we have
structurally less demand but we also
have higher inflation volatility as a
function of this higher level of
inflation and those two things are in my
opinion a direct uh uh stimulus to the

jobs acts expiring on December 31st so

chips their older chip because there

2,000 chips and instead of spending
hundreds of millions of dollars on
training the model that means just
having the model go through lots of data
cost us $6 million to do it and then you

index and you thought you being the

more competition and that's why you're
